title: Why AI Needs Nuclear Power and What India's Doing About It
meta_title: Why AI Needs Nuclear Power and What India's Doing About It | Aperture
summary: AI data centers now consume more electricity than entire countries. Here's why that's pushing the world, and India, toward nuclear power in 2026.
primary_question: Why does AI need nuclear power?
key_takeaways: A ChatGPT query uses about 10 times the electricity of a Google search; global AI data center demand may hit 1,050 TWh by 2026.
AI workloads need power every minute of every day, which solar and wind alone can't reliably deliver without expensive battery storage at scale.
The global small modular reactor pipeline nearly doubled from 25 GW to 45 GW in a single year.
India's SHANTI Act ended the state monopoly on nuclear, opening the sector to private investment and up to 49% foreign ownership for the first time.
India targets 100 GW of nuclear capacity by 2047, up from roughly 7.5 GW today, backed by a Rs 20,000 crore SMR mission.
content:
AI is hungry for power in a way that has no real precedent. A single ChatGPT query uses about 10 times the electricity of a basic Google search. Scale that across hundreds of millions of daily queries plus enormous training runs, and global data centers could collectively consume 1,050 terawatt-hours of electricity by 2026. Nuclear is the strongest candidate to meet that demand because it delivers carbon-free power constantly, without depending on the weather.

Key takeaways

  • A ChatGPT query uses about 10 times the electricity of a Google search; global AI data center demand may hit 1,050 TWh by 2026.
  • AI workloads need power every minute of every day, which solar and wind alone can't reliably deliver without expensive battery storage at scale.
  • The global small modular reactor pipeline nearly doubled from 25 GW to 45 GW in a single year.
  • India's SHANTI Act ended the state monopoly on nuclear, opening the sector to private investment and up to 49% foreign ownership.
  • India targets 100 GW of nuclear capacity by 2047, up from roughly 7.5 GW today, backed by a Rs 20,000 crore SMR mission.

Why does AI need so much electricity?

Two things drive AI's power appetite. Training is the first: building a large model requires feeding massive datasets through billions of parameters for weeks at high power density. Inference is the second: every query you send activates those billions of parameters again. Unlike a web server handling cached content, an AI server does fresh heavy computation for every single response.

That figure is sobering. In 2024, data centers already consumed around 415 terawatt-hours, more than all of Spain. The growth is accelerating, not leveling off. Sam Altman has described the need for an energy breakthrough as one of the core constraints on AI's future. He's right. The math doesn't leave much room for debate.

How much power are we actually talking about?

In 2024, global data center electricity use was roughly 415 terawatt-hours, already more than Spain uses in a year. AI growth could push that past 1,050 terawatt-hours by 2026, placing data centers between Japan and Russia in the global energy rankings.

The number that surprises most people: training a single large language model can consume as much electricity as 100 average homes use in a full year. And that's just the training run. Every response a user recieves draws more power from a server rack running near full load. The IEA's Energy and AI report found that data centre electricity use surged in 2025 even as supply bottlenecks tightened.

Why nuclear and not just solar or wind?

Nuclear plants run at roughly 90 to 93 percent of rated capacity around the clock. Solar and wind depend on weather and need large battery backups to fill gaps. For a data center that cannot go offline, nuclear is the only current low-carbon source that guarantees constant, predictable output.

Think about what that means if you're running infrastructure serving millions of users. You can't wait for the sun to rise. You can pair solar and wind with batteries, and plenty of operators do, but at hyperscale the battery storage requirment becomes enormous and expensive. Nuclear sidesteps that entirely.

FactorSmall Modular ReactorSolar + Wind + Storage
24/7 reliabilityYesRequires large battery backup
Carbon outputNear zeroNear zero
Land footprintSmallVery large
Build time3 to 5 years (SMR)1 to 2 years
Output predictabilityVery highWeather-dependent

Microsoft restarted Three Mile Island specifically to power its AI workloads. Big tech companies globally have signed conditional agreements for more than 10 gigawatts of new nuclear capacity in the past year. The global SMR pipeline nearly doubled from 25 gigawatts at end-2024 to 45 gigawatts by early 2026. The shovels aren't all in the ground yet, but the direction is set.

What India is actually doing about it

India has roughly 7.5 gigawatts of nuclear capacity today, most of it in plants running since the 1990s. The government's target is 100 gigawatts by 2047, a 13-fold increase that required a completely different policy architecture.

Parliament passed the SHANTI Act in late 2025. For decades, nuclear in India was entireley state-run. The SHANTI Act changed that. Private companies can now participate through joint ventures and public-private partnerships. Foreign investment of up to 49 percent is permitted. It's the most significant shift in Indian nuclear policy since 1962.

A Rs 20,000 crore Nuclear Energy Mission backs the development of small modular reactors. The first two planned SMRs, a 200 MW Bharat Small Modular Reactor and a 55 MW unit, are slated for Tarapur in Maharashtra by 2033. A single large data center needs 50 to 100 MW of uninterrupted power. An SMR close to the campus delivers that without stressing the regional grid.

What this means beyond the headlines

Say you're a developer or product manager based in Hyderabad. Your cloud provider runs the models you use every day. If that data center sits in India and runs on domestic nuclear power, latency drops, your data stays within Indian jurisdiction, and prices are less exposed to global fuel swings. That last point matters for healthcare and finance companies operating under data-residency rules.

There's a jobs dimension too. SMR construction needs nuclear safety engineers, civil contractors, and project managers in volumes India hasn't required in this sector for decades. Places like Tarapur and Rawatbhata are likely to see sustained investment where very little has arrived for a long time.

Will every timeline hold? Probably not exactly. Nuclear projects in every country run late. The 45 gigawatt global SMR pipeline is mostly conditional agreements, not operating reactors. But India is, for once, definately moving faster on energy policy than most observers expected, and the pressure from AI demand is not easing.

Frequently asked questions

What is a small modular reactor?

A small modular reactor is a nuclear plant rated below 300 megawatts, much smaller than conventional reactors. SMRs can be partly factory-built and deployed faster, making them practical for powering specific industrial sites or data centers. India plans its first two SMRs, rated 200 MW and 55 MW, at Tarapur by 2033.

How much electricity does a large data center use?

A large data center typically consumes 50 to 100 megawatts continuously, enough to power tens of thousands of homes. Hyperscale AI facilities can exceed 500 megawatts. GPU-heavy workloads run at high utilization around the clock, making their per-unit energy demand far larger than conventional computing.

What exactly did the SHANTI Act change?

The SHANTI Act ended India's state monopoly on nuclear power, which had existed since 1962. It allows private companies to build and run reactors through joint ventures, permits up to 49 percent foreign direct investment, and aims to speed up project approvals that historically moved very slowly.

Can AI just run on solar power instead?

Solar is cheap and produces no carbon, but it stops generating at night and in bad weather. AI servers need power every hour of every day. Battery storage to cover solar's gaps at data center scale remains very expensive. Nuclear provides the steady baseload power that solar currently cannot match cost-effectively.

The short version

AI's power appetite is growing faster than most grids can handle. Solar and wind help, but they can't deliver the around-the-clock reliability data centers need without storage that doesn't exist cheaply at scale. Nuclear fills that gap. India is betting on exactly that: a new law, a Rs 20,000 crore SMR mission, and a target of 100 gigawatts of nuclear capacity by 2047. The timelines may slip. The direction won't.
